title: The Black Obelisk: Israel's King Bows to Assyria
description: The Black Obelisk of Shalmaneser III — containing the only known contemporary image of an Israelite king. It shows Jehu (or his ambassador) of Israel bowing face-down before the Assyrian emperor, paying tribute of gold, silver, and precious objects. The earliest depiction of a biblical figure.
introduction: The Black Obelisk, discovered at Nimrud in 1846, is a four-sided limestone pillar covered in carved reliefs depicting the military campaigns and tribute-receiving ceremonies of the Assyrian king Shalmaneser III (858-824 BCE). Its importance for biblical studies cannot be overstated: one panel shows 'Jehu, son of Omri' (Jehu, king of Israel) or his ambassador prostrating himself before the Assyrian emperor — the only known contemporary image of any person named in the Hebrew Bible.

translation.text: The tribute of Jehu, son of Omri: I received from him silver, gold, a golden bowl, a golden vase with pointed bottom, golden tumblers, golden buckets, tin, a staff for a king, and spears. On the carved relief panel, a bearded figure in Israelite dress prostrates himself before Shalmaneser III. Behind the kneeling figure, Israelite attendants carry tribute. This is the only surviving contemporary depiction of any king of Israel or Judah.
